coredata.dc:title: Decorated xanthan gum/alginate mingled hydrogel beads@La(III)-MOFs@reduced graphene oxide@graphene quantum dots nanohybrid for adsorptive capture and recovery of U(VI)
coredata.dc:creator: Tharwat, Rana M.
Mahmoud, Mohamed E.
Abdelfattah, Amir M.
Hassan, Saad S.M.
coredata.dc:description: Uranium as an example of radionuclide toxic heavy metal has shown serious impacts on human and living systems causing well-known sever diseases and therefore, proper attention should be directed and given to safely store and remove uranium hazardous species from the environment especially water resources by finding effective approaches for remediation and capturing uranium from water or wastewater. Therefore, the present investigation is designed to fabricating and combining of xanthan gum and alginate mingled hydrogel beads with reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and lanthanum 1,4-benzene di-carboxylate (La-MOFs) in a single nanocomposite (La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel) for direct application in adsorptive capturing of U(VI) from water. The assembled nanocomposite was found into crystalline structure as well as characterized and detected with multiple correlated active functional groups to the constituting units from the FT-IR analysis, while the HR-TEM analysis showed uniformly distributed particles with 25.3 nm as the average nanocomposite size. The effectiveness behavior of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite in adsorptive capturing of uranyl ions (50 and 100 mg/L) was explored and optimized in a number of experimental conditions and revealed to the optimum pH = 7.12, nanocomposite dose = 20 mg and reaction time = 30 min. The adsorptive capture interaction of U(VI) by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite perfectly fitted with the linear and nonlinear pseudo-second order expressions giving 0.96 and 0.93 as well as the linear and nonlinear Freundlich isotherm expressions yielded 0.997 and 0.997 correlation factors, respectively. The computed thermodynamic parameters provided good evidence for the endothermic capture type of U(VI) interaction with La-BDC/GQDs-rGO@mingled hydrogel. The nanocomposite was subjected to more than three regeneration processes with satisfactory results. Finally, La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite achieved excellent capture percentage of U(VI) from sea, tap and wastewaters providing 91.19 %, 90.78 % and 91.53 % from 100 mg/L concentration to deliver a direct confirmation for its potential superior performance in adsorptive capturing of U(VI) from contaminated waters.

head.ce:title: Decorated xanthan gum/alginate mingled hydrogel beads@La(III)-MOFs@reduced graphene oxide@graphene quantum dots nanohybrid for adsorptive capture and recovery of U(VI)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Uranium as an example of radionuclide toxic heavy metal has shown serious impacts on human and living systems causing well-known sever diseases and therefore, proper attention should be directed and given to safely store and remove uranium hazardous species from the environment especially water resources by finding effective approaches for remediation and capturing uranium from water or wastewater. Therefore, the present investigation is designed to fabricating and combining of xanthan gum and alginate mingled hydrogel beads with reduced graphene oxide (rGO)/graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and lanthanum 1,4-benzene di-carboxylate (La-MOFs) in a single nanocomposite (La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel) for direct application in adsorptive capturing of U(VI) from water. The assembled nanocomposite was found into crystalline structure as well as characterized and detected with multiple correlated active functional groups to the constituting units from the FT-IR analysis, while the HR-TEM analysis showed uniformly distributed particles with 25.3 nm as the average nanocomposite size. The effectiveness behavior of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite in adsorptive capturing of uranyl ions (50 and 100 mg/L) was explored and optimized in a number of experimental conditions and revealed to the optimum pH = 7.12, nanocomposite dose = 20 mg and reaction time = 30 min. The adsorptive capture interaction of U(VI) by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite perfectly fitted with the linear and nonlinear pseudo-second order expressions giving 0.96 and 0.93 as well as the linear and nonlinear Freundlich isotherm expressions yielded 0.997 and 0.997 correlation factors, respectively. The computed thermodynamic parameters provided good evidence for the endothermic capture type of U(VI) interaction with La-BDC/GQDs-rGO@mingled hydrogel. The nanocomposite was subjected to more than three regeneration processes with satisfactory results. Finally, La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite achieved excellent capture percentage of U(VI) from sea, tap and wastewaters providing 91.19 %, 90.78 % and 91.53 % from 100 mg/L concentration to deliver a direct confirmation for its potential superior performance in adsorptive capturing of U(VI) from contaminated waters.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Uranium is a word which comes with major concern due to its nuclear activity and ability to generate nuclear power to replace other traditional technologies. For example, few grams of uranium provide high energy and therefore, many countries around the world get the advantages of this exciting power. However, it comes with dangerous accidents and consequences as the case of Fukushima atomic accident (Japan 2011) from which water was polluted with radioactive materials [1]. Uranium is existing in major three isotopes U-238, U-235 and U-238 with the most abundant one is U-238 and its half lifetime is 4.5 × 109 years, Uranium species may enter the environment through many aspects, for examples, but not limited to, nuclear fuel effluent, mining of uranium, weapon trials specially in cold war and nuclear accident like Fukushima nuclear accident. Uranium as a radionuclide and toxic heavy metal has serious impacts and effects on human health causing many diseases including cancer, genetic mutation, and many others as well as its effects on the ecological system [2]. Therefore, proper attention should be directed to safely store and remove uranium hazardous species from the environment especially wastewater by finding out simple and cost-effective approaches for remediation and capturing uranium from water or wastewater using sedimentation methods, liquid–liquid sorption, lime softening, membrane disengagement and adsorption process [3]. Adsorptive capture process has been emerged as a proper and successful candidate for sequestering U(VI) from aquatic environment and that owes to its simplicity, efficiency, cost effectiveness and excellent selectivity along with ease of practical transfer into industrialization [4]. Regarding this issue, U(VI) was eliminated from water matrices via oxidized multiple walled carbon nanotubes with noticeable recyclability [5]. In addition, modified multiwalled carbon nanotube with magnetic cobalt ferrite was also applied in removal of U(VI) [6]. In another approach, U(VI) was successfully eliminated by the aid of ultrafiltration via complexation with polyethylenimine [7].

MOFs (metal organic frameworks) are categorized as polymeric complexes which are consisting of organic building unit (ligand) like terephthalic acid, benzene tricarboxylic acid and many other derivatives which are coordinately or covalently attached to inorganic species (metal ion) as transition metal, lanthanides, actinides or may be generated from two or more metal ions [8]. MOFs were known as successful nanosorbents for the elimination of uranium from water matrices because of elevated surface area, pore dimension, superior specifications along with several functionalities as hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amino groups [9,10]. In this respect, modified zirconium benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid (UiO-66) by carbonylated (GO) as capturing adsorbent to uranium in sea water was recently reported [11] . Also, Fe3O4@AMCA-MIL53 as nanomagnetic MOFs were utilized to eliminate uranyl in addition to Th(VI) and referred to the acceptable efficiency of the investigated MOFs in removal of radionuclides [12]. Magnetic MIL-101 decorated amino group was investigated to eliminate uranyl and was identified to exhibit elevated sorption efficiency [13]. Azo and amido derivatives utilized to modulate MOFs to form zinc azo-MOF along with implementation in the operation of U(VI) uptake[14].

Graphene oxide and its subordinates as reduced graphene oxide besides mutated graphene oxide composites have protruded as efficient sorbents for uranium due to their distinctive 2D connections, exterior obtainable positions, physio-chemical distinctive, elevated surface area, powerful integrated linkage of combined aromatic ring with the enhanced thermal–mechanical steadiness [15]. It was detected that (GO) and its subordinates can be employed to uptake uranyl from several pHs [16]. On the other hand, GQDs are generally synthesized via regularized heating of organic matter like citric acid and were identified to show unique specifications like nontoxic, bio-appropriateness, high fluorescence, elevated photo-settlement, rigid chemical inefficiency as well as exterior obtainable functionalities like carbonyl, hydroxyl, epoxy groups in addition to carboxylates as the coordinating sites [17]. Also, GQDs have been used as detectors to distinguish various pollutants [18]. Carbon containing composites are excellent candidate in elimination operations because of its assembly characteristics besides their modulated structures and therefore, were reported as nanosorbents to eliminate uranyl by modified graphene oxide with hydroxyapatite [19]. Moreover, magnetic graphene oxide modulated by EDTA was reported to show efficient removal process of uranium [20], while (rGO) was also utilized to the aim of uranyl elimination by using reduced graphene oxide-decorated-zero valent iron [21].

Naturalistic and non-poisonous polysaccharides natural polymers have been widely utilized to prepare various environment friendly sorbents, energy storage and natural medicines [22]. Hydrogels derived from polysaccharide were employed in water remediation because of their elevated uptake capacity, quick uptake rate, besides good reusability [23]. Otherwise, polysaccharide hydrogels are known to afford several attainable reactive positions as carboxylates, amides, sulfonates groups as well as hydroxyls and these are capable of capturing of cations from polluted water [24]. Example of these is xanthan gum which is produced from (Xanthomonas campestris) microorganism and as adsorbent was modified to the hydrogel for the aim of heavy metals removal from water [25]. Similarly, sodium alginate is obtained from marine Phaeophyceae in the form 1,4 linked d-mannuronic acid and therefore, is characterized with good capability of pollutants removal [26]. For example, calcium linked alginate hydrogel was reported as an efficient sorbent to capture toxic metals [27]. However, ordinary hydrogels were reported to show unsettled physical integration which block their utilization in a convoluted environments due to the enhancement of degeneration and gelation operation as disadvantages [28]. Therefore, modulation of naturalistic polymers by organic and inorganic materials achieved excellent elimination efficiency which is not only relying on the chemical specifications of their derivatives but also based on the direct cooperation among these constituents [29]. Fabrication of alginate and xanthan gum hydrogels relies on the powerful intermolecular power to produce more complicated hydrogel network matrices. For example, silica modulated calcium alginate and xanthan gum mixed hydrogel beads composite was used for the capturing of Pb(II) [30].

Despite the wide spread applications of hydrogel materials in elimination of diverse contaminates, especially heavy metals, but limited studies have been declared for capturing of uranyl from water matrices [31]. Moreover, the combination of xanthan gum and alginate mingled hydrogel beads with GO, rGO and La-MOFs in a single nanocomposite was not previously reported for adsorptive capturing of U(VI) from aqueous medium. Therefore, the current study is aimed and designed to link the above five constituents in a novel nanomaterial (La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads). This strategy permits to get befits of each interlinked component. For example, the mingled hydrogel is identified with the occurrence of a net formula along with a large quantity of hydroxyl active sites because of the occurrence of basal xanthan gum in addition to alginate to permit powerful elimination of U(VI) from aqueous medium. GQDs and rGO are two examples of highly appreciated carbonaceous material with very low particle size and two-dimensional structure to provide high surface area magnitude of various functionalities for capturing of U(VI). In addition, La-BDC MOF is an example of elevated porous composites with good tendency for binding with U(VI) via pore filling mechanism and other modes of linkage.

Uranyl acetate (F.W. 424.15 g.mol−1, 98 %), (C6H9NaO7, Xanthan gum (C35H49O29), lanthanum nitrate ((La(NO3) 3.6H₂O, FW 433.02 g.mol−1, 99 %), benzene-1,4-dicarboxylic acid (H2BDC, F.W. 166.3 g.mol−1, 99 %) and hydrazine monohydrate (NH2NH2·H2O, F.W. 50.06 g.mol−1, ≥98) were all purchased from Sigma-Aldrich, USA. Na2CO3 (F.W.105.9888 g.mol−1, 99.88 %), Sodium chloride (F.W. 58.44 g.mol−1, ˃ 99 %), potassium chloride (F.W.74.55 g.mol−1, 99 %), sodium hydroxide (F.W. 40 g.mol−1, 98 %), sodium nitrate (F.W. 84.995 g.mol−1, 98 %), hydrochloric acid (F.W. 36.46 g.mol−1, 37 %) in addition to graphite powder were obtained by WINLAB Ltd., England·H2O2 (F.W. 34.015 g.mol−1, 30 %), calcium chloride (F.W. 110.98 g.mol−1, ˃99 %), H2SO4 (F.W. 98.079 g.mol−1, 98 %), potassium permanganate (F.W. 158.04 g.mol−1, 99 %), H3PO4 (F.W. 97.994 g.mol−1, 85 %), (C6H8O6, F.W.176.124 g.mol−1, 30 %), (C3H7NO, F.W.73.09 g.mol−1, 99.8 %) in addition to (C₆H₈O₇, F.W.192.10 g.mol−1, 99.6 %) were obtained by VWR Ltd, Poole, England.

Absorbance of uranyl acetate was identified with UV–visible instrument 2700. The mechanical stirring process was done with an automatic vibrator using 150 rpm. Controlled pH-meter Adwa by several buffers was employed to regulate uranyl acetate pH magnitudes. BRUKER VERTEX FT-IR 70 in 400 to 4500 cm−1 was utilized to obtain the FTIR spectrum of the adsorbents. JEOL-JFC-1100E was utilized to collect the SEM images. JEOL-JSM-1400 Plus, Japan employed for detecting the TEM images. TGA-50-Schimadzu, Japan was employed for acquiring the thermal gravimetric analysis (TGA) as well as the XRD were analyzed by Shimadzu lab, Japan.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis approach of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads
ce:section.ce:para: La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads were synthesized following five steps. (i) Graphene oxide (GO) was first prepared with modified Hummer’sprocedure [32]. Concisely, graphite powder (5.0 g) was added to 2.0 g sodium nitrate, 12.0 mL H3PO4 besides 108 mL H2SO4 in 1.0 L beaker and the mixture was vibrated for 10 min in ice bath. Further,15.0 g potassium permanganate was progressively combined with the mixture maintaining the temperature below 5 °C with stirring for one hour. Posteriorly, the mixture was vibrated for additional 1.0 h at 40 °C and afterwards, vibrated at 98 °C for 1 h. After that DDW was gradually mixed until the volume became 400 mL and 20 mL of H2O2 posteriorly mixed. The resulted GO material was centrifuged and washed with HCl (5 %), DW till pH 7 and dried for 12 h at 70 °C [33]. (ii) To prepare reduced graphene oxide (rGO), GO (4.0 g) was mixed with DW (100 mL), vibrated for half hour,whereas the pH was controlled at pH 10 by 0.1 M sodium hydroxide and mixed for half hour. Eventually, 80 mL of N2H4 was combined then refluxed (8 h) and the formed (rGO) was rinsed with DW and desiccated for half day at 70 °C [34]. (iii) To fabricate (GQD), 2.0 g of C₆H₈O₇ was placed in a 50 mL beaker then heated up for half hour at 200 °C. At primer 5 min of carbonization, C₆H₈O₇ was transformed into yellow color and progressively changed into deep orange posterior half hour. Eventually, carbonized C₆H₈O₇ was mixed with 10 g/L sodium hydroxide and vibrated and the blend was desiccated in a furnace at 70 °C [35]. (iv) La-BDC/rGO/GODs was prepared via this method. In a ¼ L beaker, 0.84 g H2BDC was mixed with 1.0 g of lanthanum nitrate, 20 mL of C3H7NO then vigorously vibrated for 20 min and this solution was then added to a 250 mL beaker containing 1.0 g reduced graphene oxide,1.0 g graphene quantum dots and 20 mL C3H7NO. Eventually the blend was refluxed for 2 h at 70 °C and it was allowed to heat in a Teflon padded hydrothermal autoclave at 120 °C for 24 h. (v) The aimed nanocomposite of decorated xanthan gum/alginate mingled hydrogel beads@La(III)-MOFs@reduced graphene oxide@graphene quantum dots nanohybrid (La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel) was assembled on the basis of the following approach. 3.0 g of La-BDC/rGO/GODs was added to 300 mL deionized water containing 3.0 g of Na-alginate and 3.0 g of xanthan gum and steady stirred till analogous blend was formed. This was added to a Petri dish with 20 mL of 0.5 M calcium chloride utilizing 10.0 mL syringe, at which the hydrogel beads were shaped and left in calcium chloride solution for one day. Finally, the beads were washed by DW then dried at 60 °C [36]. Scheme 1 demonstrates a direct illustration for the formation of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite.

Uranyl acetate (stock solution, 1000 mg/L) was made and diluted to produce 50 as well as 100 mg/L concentrations and the detected absorbance was established at λmax 360 nm with spectrophotometer instrument. The chromogenic agent for uranyl detection was made by mixing 70 mL of 1.0 M C6H8O6, 40 mL 0.5 M Na2CO3 in addition to 90 mL DW (deionized water). The uptake operations were done at ordinary room temperature in addition to the adsorption reactions were accomplished with an automatic shaker. Adsorptive uptake of uranyl onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite was conducted out under various experimental characteristics involving the influence of pH, La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite mass, interaction capture duration, U(VI) concentration, ionic strength, and reaction capture temperature as detailed in following methods.

(i) The influence of interaction pH on the capturing process of uranyl by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite was established by mixing 20 mg of the hydrogel beads with 10 mL uranyl (50 & 100 mg/L) in different pH conditions ranging from 2 to 9. The pH of U(VI) was controlled via 0.1 M sodium hydroxide and hydrochloric acid as desired and these solutions were automatically vibrated for half hour. After completion, these solutions were filtered out and the free concentrations of U(VI) were detected and the (%E) capturing values were computed from Eq. (1). In addition, the measurement of point zero charge (PZC) was done by mixing a 0.1 g La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite with KCl solution (40 mL of 0.01 M) and pH was adjusted at 2–11 with 0.1 M of sodium hydroxide or hydrochloric acid. The mixture was mechanically vibrated at 4 h and the final pHs after 12 h were determined. The PZC was detected via sketching the plot of ΔpH (pHfinal-pHinitial) against pH [30]. (ii) The influence of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads dose on the capturing process of U(VI) was investigated by mixing 10 mL uranyl 50, 100 mg/L concentrations with various doses of nanocompsite from 5 mg to 100 mg at neutral pH and shaking for half hour. These mixtures were filtered and eventually the free U(VI) was then determined to compute the (%E) removal according to Eq. (1). (iii) The effectiveness of contact duration in capturing process of uranyl by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite was investigated by using added 20 mg of nanocomposite to10 mL of 50, 100 mg/Luranyl concentrations at neural pH and vibrated at different time intervals (2–60 min). These solutions were then filtered and eventually the residual uranyl was determined to calculate the (%E) recovery according to Eq. (1). (iv) The influence of initial uranyl concentration on the adsorptive capturing process by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite was evaluated by shaking 10 mL uranyl concentrations (50–100 mg/L) together with 20 mg La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite in pH7 for half hour. The free U(VI) was then detected to calculate the (%E) adsorptive capturing according to Eq. (1). (v) The ionic strength influence on uranyl capturing onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite was examined by using various masses of 10–100 mg NaCl via addition to 20 mg nanocomposite. The investigated NaCl masses were added and mechanically stirred with 10 mL (50 & 100 mg/L uranyl) at neutral pH for half hour and eventually the free U(VI) was then determined to compute the (%E) recovery according to Eq. (1). (vi) The influence of U(VI) adsorptive capturing temperature by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was identified via mixing and shaking 20 mg of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel with 10 mL (50 & 100 mg/L uranyl) at neutral pH for half hour at several temperatures range 25–60 °C, and the free uranyl was then detected to elucidate the (%E) capturing according to Eq. (1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reusability of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads and application on real water
ce:section.ce:para: For recycling and reuse of the previously investigated and loaded La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite with U(VI), the following procedure was performed. 50 mg/L uranyl was added to the nanocomposite and mechanically stirred for half an hour. Posteriorly filtered, recycled by 100 mL 0.1 M HCl and washed with DDW until the pH became neutral. The recycled nanocomposite was desiccated at 70 °C then subjected to additional reuse by treatment 20 mg with 50 mg/L uranium (VI) (10 mL), vibrated for half an hour and eventually, the free U(VI) was determined to identify the (%E) removal according to Eq. (1). This process was repeated more than four times to confirm the stability of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite.

The prospective utilization of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads nanocomposite in adsorptive capturing and eliminating uranyl from actual water specimens viz. waste, sea and tap waters was also explored in this research work by spiked samples with 50 and100 mg/L uranyl. 10 mL of the selected uranyl solution was mixed with 20 mg La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel, while the pH was controlled to 7 and the mixtures were vibrated for 30 min. Eventually, the free uranium (VI) was detected to characterize the (%E) recovery based on Eq. (1).

FTIR analysis is used to help in interpretation of the exterior functional groups. As GO FTIR as clarified (Fig. 1a ), it is characterized by various bands at 3404 cm−1 confirming OH group expansion of carboxylate, band at 1717 cm−1 referring to CO group, 1236 cm−1 indicating COH stretching, 1055 cm−1 for ether vibration, besides 1583 cm−1 indicating the SP2 hybridized alkene. Therefore, these oxygenated sites are confirming the accomplished conversion into GO [37]. The FT-IR of produced rGO was identified with a decline in the matchmaking of bands compared with that of GO to confirm occurrence of reduction operation. For example, the band at 3436 cm−1 was exhibited at minimized intensity, whilst the CO group peak 1716 cm−1 mainly disappeared and the COH peak at 1235 cm−1 was also vanished. Moreover, the peaks 1575 cm−1 is corresponding to CC in phenolic ring stretching, 1412 cm−1 indicating bending CH oscillation in addition to 1109 cm−1 referring to epoxide CO expanding [38]. The GQDs FT-IR declared that the bands at 3469 cm−1 for hydroxyl oscillation and linked water particles. The peak at 1654 cm−1 is matching with carbonyl oscillation, whilst 1561 cm−1,1401 cm−1 are pointing out to the stretching symmetrical and asymmetrical carboxylate group oscillation. La-BDC MOF declared the peak at 3448 cm−1 for OH vibration of COOH, the 1542 cm−1 and 1393 cm−1 bands indicating symmetrical and asymmetrical of CO oscillation which confirm the linkage of La ions with H2BDC ligand [39] and, the peak at 752 cm−1 is matching with the La-O bond oscillation pointing out to the successful formation of MOF [40]. Eventually, La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads exhibited to some peaks at 3352 cm−1 to OH in carboxylate,1604 cm−1 in carbonyl,1408 cm−1 to confirm the vibration of carboxylate to refer to the participation of sodium alginate in the hydrogel formation with xanthan gum and other components [41]. Finally, the 1019 cm−1 for COC vibration in addition to 742 cm−1 for LaO bond vibration confirm the successful formation of MOF in cooperation with other constituents forming the beads as illustrated in Fig. 1a.

The XRD diffraction angles in La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads represented in Fig. 1b and it is characterized by a good crystalline formulation with sharp and intense peaks 2Θ = 31.6°, 45.3°, 56.7°, 66.8° and 75.9° due to the contribution of La-BDC, rGO, and GQDs hydrogel [42–44]. In addition, the weak peaks at less than 27.0 are mainly attributed to the prepared xanthan gum and alginate mingled hydrogel. As illustrated in Fig. 1c , XPS is generally applied for determining the intensity of dispersed photoelectrons of various particles. The XPS scanning of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads is showing the exterior atomic composition of C 1S with elemental ratio 53.5 %, O 1S 22.4 %, Cl 2P3 14.15 %, Ca 2p 5.75 %, La 3d5 0.8 % and N 1S with atomic percentage 3.39 %. The XPS of C 1S assigned the binding energy values at 284.27 eV which is corresponding to C SP2 of rGO,289 eV of COOH and 286 eV to C-OH. The binding energy of O1S 531.38 eV are devoted to CO, besides 533 eV band to COH [45]. The binding energy peaks of Ca 2p correspond to major Ca 2p3/2 band with 347. 64 eV and secondary peak of Ca 2p1/2 with 350.85 eV [46]. The bands of La 3d5 were found at 835.83 eV, 839.15 eV, 855.64 eV and 851.97 eV with the ascribed band at 835.83 eV to the coordination between lanthanum and the ligand. The presence of calcium ions and chloride ions as calcium chloride is related to the hydrogel cross-linker at 200.15 eV, 198.08 eV, 201.25 eV and 199.11 eV, whilst the band at 400.29 eV are 401.55 eV for N 1S are due to the applied hydrazine monohydrate in GO reduction which is related to primary amine and protonated ammonium ion [47]. In addition, small dribbles of sodium are existing and appearing at 1073.32 eV band (Fig. 1c).

The illustrated TGA thermogram of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads in Fig. 1d shows a high weight loss at 111.2 °C (loss 0.514 mg and 25.3 %) due to the evaporization of water molecules from this nanocomposite and possible evaporation of some water molecules from the hydrogel. At 214.7 °C, the mass decline was 0.121 mg (5.9 %) as the oxygen inclusive sets like OH as well as COOH in rGO, GQDs in addition to alginate hydrogel were decayed to CO and CO2 [48]. At 351.1 °C, the mass decline was increased to 0.287 mg by a percentage of 14.1 % due to decomposed oxygen inclusive groups constantly as well as the decay of ligand in LaBDC MOF. At temperatures 583.2 °C, the weight loss was 0.154 mg (7.45 % loss) due to the decomposition of carbon in the shell of rGO and ligand in LaBDC MOF continued to decay slowly. Above 583.2 °C, the decay produced Lanthanum oxide as illustrated in Fig. 1d [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorptive capturing studies of uranium (VI) onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
The capture of uranyl by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was performed by the batch capture process which is established on measuring the percentage of U(VI) (%E) from the absorbance of uranyl ion [50] as clarified in the equation (1).
(1)
% E =
C o
-
C e
C o
× 100

The value of capture of uranyl at settlement was measured from equation (2).
(2)
q e
=
C o
-
C e
W / V
Co and Ce refer to starting & final concentrations of U(VI), whilst V (L) and W (g).

The pH domain 2.32–9.52 was selected to investigate and approve the influence of pH on the adsorptive capture efficiency of uranium (VI) onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite. It is evident from Fig. 2a that the capture capacity at pH ≈ 2 was 46 % and 55.1 % at 100 mg/L as well as 50 mg/L concentrations, correspondingly. Beneath pH 4 the existing U(VI) cations are basically in the formula UO2 2+, (UO2)4(OH)7 + and UO2OH+ as well as, the surface charge of nanocomposite is positive to yield declined efficiency in acidic pH as clarified at Fig. 2a. By elevating the pH, the capture percentage increased to the maximum value at pH 7.12 providing 79.77 % and 86.18 % using 100 mg/L & 50 mg/L concentrations, correspondingly as UO2(OH)2 0 as well as (UO2)2(OH)4 2− forms started to dominate with slight presence of UO2OH+ and (UO2)2(OH)+ to confirm that La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite can link to both due to the different active sites on the nanocomposite as oxygen containing functional groups. With further increasing the pH value, the capture capacity was found to decline to reach to the minimum values at 60.8 % and 65.85 % using pH 9.59 at which uranyl ions were transferred into the more dominated hydrolyzed forms as (UO2)3(OH)10 4−, (UO2)(CO3)(OH)3 4−, UO2(CO3)2 2–, UO2 (CO3)3 4−, UO2 (CO3)4−, and UO2 (CO3)3 4− [51]. likewise, at elevated pH the alginate –COOH group, besides oxygenated functional sits at both alginate and xanthan gum in addition to several oxygen containing active sites in GQDs as well as MOFs were found promising nominees to form complexes in addition to ion-pair to asset the uptake capture of uranyl [52].

The PZC is an experimental mode utilized to identify the nanocomposite surface charge. The PZC magnitude is defined as the pH of net charge at which the superficies of nanocomposite is zero. The La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite PZC was determined as pH 6 to confirming the nanocomposite over all charge was zero such clarified in Fig. 2b . Beneath pH 6, the exterior charge is totally positive and over pH 6, the exterior was mainly negative to assure maximum binding with U(VI) ions [53].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite dose.
ce:section.ce:para: The dosage of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite is a significance factor for the capturing operation of uranyl. Thus, a wide domain of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel from 5 −50 mg was utilized to exploring the efficiency of such factor. The results are illustrated in Fig. 2c and refer that by elevating the mass of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel, the ratio of uranyl capture, would be also enhanced to emphasize and refer to the high obtainable positions on nanocomposite surface till reaching the settlement condition. The maximum achieved capture of U(VI) were 68.87 % and 80.73 % upon utilizing 30 mg weight La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite with uranyl concentrations corresponding to 100 and 50 mg /L,correspondingly [54].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The influence of capturing reaction duration and kinetics investigations.
ce:section.ce:para: The influence of reaction duration on adsorptive capture capacity of uranyl by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was investigated using 2–60 min. It was recognized that with enhancing the interaction duration, the capture capacity was gradually elevated then it languid until attaining the settlement at half an hour. Primarily, the vacant reactive sites at La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel surface were slowly loaded with the uranyl till attaining the settlement condition, when all the reactive positions were filled with uranyl and therefore, no more increace in the capture capacity. As illustrated in Fig. 2d , the uranyl concentration 100 & 50 mg/L were detected to reach the capture percentage 34.31 %, 40.53 % at two min, respectively. Alternatively, at 30 min optimum time, the capture percentages reached the highest capacities at 78.033 % and 79.65 % for 100 and 50 mg/L, correspondingly [55].

The intraparticle diffusion pattern presumed that uranyl capture technique relies at two essential stages. The primer stride is established on uranyl diffusion from the solution to (La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite external laminate, whilst the final stride is the uranium (VI) expansion within the La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite holes [54]. Accordingly, the capture capacity of uranyl is depending on t1/2 instead of time. The plot at Fig. 3c shows kid corresponding to slope and C to intercept, while the R2 magnitudes for uranyl 100 and 50 mg /L concentrations were found at 0.9843 and 0.9763, respectively. Finally, Elovich model is described by congruous to configuration including asymmetrical exteriors and suitablilty for depicting chemisorption reactions [58]. In Figs. 3d, a line was produced with 1/β corresponding to the slope, whilst 1/β ln(βα) corresponding to the intercept. Identified R2 were 0.9079 & 0.8926 to uranyl 100 & 50 mg /L, correspondingly.

From the above results, it can be estimated that the uranyl adsorptive capture interaction onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was perfectly fitted with pseudo-second order model. Also, the uptake operation was directly dependent on both U(VI) and the nanocomposite confirming the adsorptive capture reaction is based on a chemisorption process. For obvious assessment of kinetic investigations, nonlinear pseudo second order, pseudo first order, intraparticle diffusion, in addition to Elovich were examined as clarified at Figs. 3e and 3f
, besides the computed magnitudes were also compiled in Table 2. It is clear that the nonlinear kinetic evaluations were highly matched to linear kinetic studies besides, corroborating the superior convenience of the pseudo second order expression.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of uranyl concentration and adsorption isotherms.
ce:section.ce:para: The uranyl concentration effectiveness (50–100 mg/L) on the capture capacity was also investigated. The adsorptive capture ratio declined by the increase in uranyl concentration because of the decrease in number of obtainable positions versus uranyl ions. This could cause a lesser number of binding interactions between the La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel superficies and U(VI) ions. Despite declining in the capturing quality, the magnitude of up taken uranyl per weight unit would grow with increasing of uranyl concentration owing to the expanding in the uranyl dynamic energy which increase the binding of uranyl ion on La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads superficies [59].

Linear in addition to nonlinear isotherm capturing expressions as Langmuir, Freundlich, Dubinin–Rasushkevich and Temkin were examined to defining the conceivable reaction mechanisms among uranyl and La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite as presented in Table 3 . Langmuir expression by linear adsorption isotherm was utilized to account for single laminate capturing interaction on a regular exterior with no linkage among the captured species [60]. Fig. 4a provides a line with elevated R2 estimates 0.9951 to affirm that Langmuir pattern could depict the capturing interaction of uranyl ions onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite. The slope besides intercept were employed for assessment of qmax as well as b (Table 4 ). The separation factor (RL) was used to estimate the sort of capture mechanism. It is commonly known that RL equal zero confirms an irreversible operation, RL greater than one refers to an undesirable operation, whilst the RL equal one, suggests a linear operation as presented in Table 3. The related values to RL were found 0.0886–0.0463 to indicate adequate capturing operation of uranyl ions onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite [60]. The Freundlich expression presumed multilayer capturing operation to occur on asymmetrical exterior that comprise diverse obtainable positions with various powers. Fig. 4b represents 1/n as slope, besides lnKf as intercept providing R2 = 0.9976 as stated (Table 4) [61]. The Temkin expression presumed that the uranyl capture heat was decreased directly with enhancing the nanocomposite exterior coverage as the integration among nanocomposite and uranyl. Fig. 4c resulted in a line with B as slope besides B lnaT as intercepts (Table 4). The R2 for uranyl capture by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was found 0.9916 [62a]. The (D-R) expression was examined to explicate the capture reaction of uranyl ions onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite, the porosity, besides the capture power (Es). The graph in Fig. 4d generated a line with Kad as slope, besides lnqs as intercept and the collected R2 was 0.8304. The estimate of Es was greater than 16 (kJ mol−1) indicates a chemisorption reaction, and the estimate of Es less than 8 (kJ mol−1) confirms a physisorption technique [62b]. Corresponding with the listed information in Table 4. Es was estimated as 0.5 kJ mol−1 for uranyl ions capturing onto La-BDC/GQDs/GO@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite to reveal to a physisorption process.

The outlined results in Table 4 interpret that Freundlich isotherm was mostly convenient to estimate the adsorptive capture interaction of uranyl onto La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite [63]. Four nonlinear isotherm patterns were employed as clarified by Fig. 4e and their estimates are stated at Table 5 . Nonlinear-Langmuir referred to R2 0.9596 with extreme capture efficiency 37.95 mg/g, whilst nonlinear Freundlich show R2 0.9973 and n = 0.248. In nonlinear Temkin, R2 0.9916 was computed with high bT of 327.77 J.mol−1 indicating strong interaction between the two interaction species. The nonlinear D-R showed correlation factor R2 = 0.977 as clarified at Fig. 4e and Table 5. Accordingly, these (R2) estimates of linear-nonlinear adsorption isotherm expressions provided that La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite could efficiently capture U(VI) ions according to Freundlich adsorption isotherm.

Several additions of NaCl (10–100 mg) were mixed with uranyl solution to investigate and determine the ionic strength influence on the adsorptive capturing process by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite as clarified in Fig. 5 . It is apparent that when 10 mg NaCl was added to U(VI) solution, an inconsiderable decline in the capture efficiency of uranyl was detected providing 70.38 % and 81.56 % to 100 and 50 mg/L, correspondingly. By adding 100 mg of NaCl salt, the uptake percentage of uranyl decreased to 27.73 % and 36.88 % which are mainly caused by the direct competition for active obtainable sites on the surface of La-BDC/GQDs/rGO@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite [54].

Adsorptive capture temperature of uranyl by La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was testified at 25–60 °C in order to detect the temperature effectiveness on the percentage uptake of 50 and 100 mg/L uranyl as shown in Fig. 6a . Endothermal adsorptive capture process of U(VI) was obtained due to the impact of increasing temperature on the uptake values of uranyl which were proportionally detected to be elevated because of evolving of the association rate of uranyl with La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel beads superficies [64]. The standard (ΔS°), (ΔH°) and (ΔG°) values are calculated from equations (3–5).

The treated and combined La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite with uranyl ions was subjected to regeneration with 100 mL 0.1 M HCl and deionized water until the pH turned into 7 and the effectiveness results of adsorptive elimination of uranyl by the regenerated nanocomposite was studied as compiled in Table 7 . The collected results referred to an inconsiderable decline at the capture ratio (84.79 %) posterior the primer cycle. The reusability operation was reiterated four times as listed out in Table 7. It could be noted that the La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite have acceptable effectiveness in capturing of U(VI) as it could be reused for three rounds with small decline in its capacity [66,67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Utilization of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite in uranyl adsorptive capturing from water
ce:section.ce:para: Several real waters viz. sea, tap and waste samples were collected and utilized to check the adsorptive capturing of uranyl by the investigated La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite. The uranyl concentration (50 mg/ L) attained the subsequent capture 92.81 %, 92.7 % and 93.29 % for tap, sea and waste waters, whilst the uranyl concentration (100 mg/L) exhibited the following capture percentages 90.67 %, 90.13 % and 92.59 % for tap, sea and wastewater samples. Moreover, the preceding mentioned results assured that the assembled La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite is convenient for utilization in adsorptive capturing of uranyl from real water specimens with high efficiency to refer that this nanomaterial will be beneficial in water treatment.

The explored La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite was characterized and confirmed from the FT-IR analysis to contain several reactive functionalities due to the incorporated constituent units from La-BDC MOF, GQDs and rGO as well as the mingled xanthan gum and alginate hydrogel beads. The XRD diffraction angles referred to a crystalline formula with several peaks of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite. The HR-TEM analysis showed uniformly distributed particles with 25.3 nm average size. The effectiveness of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite in adsorptive capturing of uranyl ions (50 and 100 mg/L) was explored to list out the optimum pH 7.12, reaction time 30 min and nanocomposite dose 20 mg. The adsorptive capture interaction of U(VI) was perfectly fitted with the linear and nonlinear pseudo-second order expressions. Moreover, these isotherm expressions revealed that La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite could be efficiently capture U(VI) ions according to Freundlich adsorption isotherm. The thermodynamic study and computed parameters provided more evidence for the endothermic capture type of interaction between U(VI) and La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite. The evaluated nanocomposite was applicably recycled for more than three rounds with satisfactorily results. Finally, the potential implication of La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite in adsorptive capturing and recovery of uranyl using 100 mg/L concentration from sea, tap and waste samples yielded 90.67 %, 90.13 % and 92.59 %, respectively. As compiled in Table 8 , a comparison of the as-synthesized nanocomposite versus other adsorbents [11,68–71] was established to deliver a good indication about the superiority in performance of the evaluated La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite. Therefore, the outcomes from this study confirm the suitability of the investigated La-BDC/rGO/GQDs@mingled hydrogel nanocomposite for future potential applications in uptake and capturing of uranyl ions from diverse real water matrices and resources with expected high percentage recovery greater than 90.0 % as indicated.
